turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first today in Case No. 14簫1504, Wittman v. Personhuballah. Mr. Carvin.

Here, when it's conceded by the plaintiffs that everything we did made perfect sense if everybody in District 3 was white, we know that they wouldn't have led to a dramatic exodus of Democratic voters into the four adjacent districts, all of which had Republican incumbents, if all of those people were white. turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: How did you 簫簫

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Right. I think it's very difficult to discern motive in a multimember legislative body, which is why this Court has always looked, for example, in Cromartie II, what are the effects? If, for example, there was a way of achieving the political objectives without respect to the racial composition of the enacted district, then it should be simple as pie for plaintiffs to come in with an alternative that doesn't rely on race that achieves their legitimate political objectives, and yet plaintiffs in the district court here have proved that any realteration of District 3 which results in a diminution of the black voting簫age population would be absolutely contrary to the normal political agenda that would be motivating the legislature if everybody involved was white. The plaintiff's alternative only reduced the BVAP by 3 percent to 50 percent, and yet that converted District 2 with a brand簫new Republican incumbent from a toss簫up district to what plaintiffs themselves characterize as a heavily Democratic district. The Court's remedy demonstrates that the reduction was to 45 percent. And what did that do? It dismantled entirely District 3 and District 4. It took away about half the districts.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Because they're asserting the constitutional right. And as this Court held in Hayes, certainly these people in adjacent districts are injured; they are just not injured in a way that's cognizable under the Fourteenth Amendment, because their personal right to discrimination has not been validated. But we are defendants appealing an adverse order. Defendants appealing an adverse order never argue that the adverse order violates their constitutional rights. This Court has made it clear repeatedly, in ASARCO, Swann, Electronic Fittings, that obviously, if the remedial order puts the defendants in a worse position than they were, they have a direct stake in the outcome of the appeal and they can appeal. turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Meese v. Keene, where the Court found that because they had attached the propaganda label to this California Democratic legislator's acid rain documentary, that hurt his chances for re簫election. turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Okay. No, I 簫簫 I apologize. But I 簫簫 I do want to make 簫簫 eliminate any confusion with Justice Kagan. turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

Thornton, this Court held it was unconstitutional to make it more difficult for incumbents to be reelected because they had to engage in mail簫in campaigns rather than be on the ballot. turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

It said, did that racial purpose have some kind of effect on the 簫簫

We're just 簫簫 this it one district, we're just going to segregate all the African簫American voters in this district. We're doing that primarily because of racial reasons. We don't like African簫American voters, and we're just going to keep them all in one district. And secondarily, that has politically簫beneficial consequences for us.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. So then 簫簫 then you're 簫簫 then it seems to me you're changing your argument because in my hypothetical, both of these things run together. They're not in conflict with each other.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So you're saying the 簫簫 the critical question is not conflict. I had thought that you thought that the critical question was conflict. Rather, the critical question is which is the primary motivation or which is the but簫for purpose, and which is the secondary motivation, even if both run in line with each other. That's a different kind of test.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: No. I'm 簫簫 I'm happy to clarify, Justice Kagan. If they're completely co簫extensive, if as here, the only way to accomplish your incumbency protection in political purposes was by doing where race predominated, then obviously it can't be the but簫for cause. If 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This is 簫簫 this is 簫簫 it's very clear, just as it is in this case I have to say, they have a list of criteria, and number one on the list is race. And then we have a lot of direct evidence in my hypothetical that this is for the most heinous racial purposes imaginable.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And the question is, does the fact that it also has political benefits, does that insulate these line drawers from what you would think is the obvious conclusion, which is this is unconstitutional conduct?

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: But 簫簫 but 簫簫 but 簫簫

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: Great. Let's assume that he picked 55 percent BVAP out of the air and just wanted to make that his top priority. Does that have any effect on district lines? If it is undisputed and clear, as it is here, that they would have drawn the district precisely the same way to protect incumbents and politics, that if you diminish below that BVAP floor, even to 50 percent, we know to a certainty because plaintiffs have proved to us that that would 簫簫 that would 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That 簫簫 that sounds to me as though it's a harmless error rule for racial discrimination. And we've never had a harmless error rule for racial discrimination. What we've said is, look, we just found racially簫discriminatory purpose, end of case.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

Cromartie held, as a matter of law, the fact that there was racial percentages, lack of compactness, and breaking of county lines is insufficient as a matter of law to find a violation. Why? Because there is an equally plausible explanation, which is politics. And it is the plaintiffs' demanding burden to prove race rather than politics.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes, but 簫簫

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: And we 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Could I ask a question, which is really highlighted by Justice Kagan's hypothetical? Because normally, were 簫簫 were it not for the Voting Rights Act, there would be a very simple answer to all of these questions, and that is that you cannot take race in account at all. It's invidious discrimination to take governmental action on the basis of race. Does Shelby County have any relevance to this case? Is this the type of case that will never come up again in the future if the Voting Rights Act is not amended?

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN

And they had to get preclearance by the Justice Department in record time, so they needed to get very quick preclearance, which is why it made eminently good sense not to go 簫簫 go below the benchmark BVAP. So it's not like employment in another context. turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: I 簫簫 I must respectfully disagree that that's a remotely implausible interpretation of this language. "In a case such as this one," comma, "where majority簫minority districts are at issue and where racial identification correlates highly with political affiliation" was somehow sending some implicit signal that what we meant was some 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: No. It's just in a case such as this one, the case before us.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: No, it's 簫簫 it's 簫簫 the beginning 簫簫

Without any further questions, I'll yield the floor.

turns.text: I did. I 簫簫

This 簫簫 not one Justice dissented from the holding in Meese v. Keene that injury to 簫簫 to candidacy was an adequate Article III injury. But they 簫簫 we think they do have standing to maintain their 簫簫

turns.text: Well, I don't want to impute 簫簫 impugn in any respect the motives of the Commonwealth of Virginia, but if it were the case that a State decided not to defend the Constitution, not to defend the legality of a districting plan that was adopted by the legislature, and that decision was made purely for partisan reasons, you would say that a number of 簫簫 that 簫簫 that an elected official or a candidate who was severely adversely affected by that should not be able to challenge it.

turns.text: That is 簫簫 that is correct, Your Honor. Not every injury in our society opens up the courthouse door. There are 簫簫 as the colloquy earlier discussed, there has to be a legally protected interest. And members of Congress do not have a legally protected interest to choose their voters.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: What if the 簫簫 the decision 簫簫 and, again, this is not a 簫簫 I'm not saying this is about Virginia in this case. But what if it were the case that that decision was made for a racist reason?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: I'm sorry. I'm not 簫簫

turns.text: What if it were made for a racist decision? What if the reason for not defending the legality of this 簫簫 of the districting plan was a racist reason on the part of the State executive? You would say that an adversely affected member of Congress or candidate may not have standing?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, because members of Congress simply don't have a legal interest in choosing their voters. It's 簫簫 it's worth looking at the supplemental briefing and the briefing on standing in this case to illustrate why the injury here is not just 簫簫 not legally protected but is entirely speculative. The State of Virginia in their initial brief, when we were asked to brief the question of standing, said that there was no particular congressmen that had standing at that time but that one may soon reveal themselves. Seven days later they filed a brief saying they have now revealed him themselves and this 簫簫 the member withstanding is Congressman Rigell, because it looks like Congressman Rigell's district is going to be affected. A month later the State of Virginia said, it's not Congressman Rigell. It's Congressman Forbes. This Court has said one needs to have standing at every stage of the proceeding. These plaintiffs needed 簫簫 I'm sorry. These Appellants needed standing at the moment they filed their appeal and at every stage thereafter.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If the State had a plan designed to protect incumbency and it did not do that, could a 簫簫 a voter object to the fact that the incumbency rationale was not followed?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: So 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Can voters assert an interest in preserving incumbency?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: So two 簫簫 two answers, Your Honor. Number one is, I don't believe an individual voter would have anything more than a general grievance. Number two, it's worth noting, just as a 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So then 簫簫 so then you have an 簫簫 an acknowledged interest on the part of this legislature, but 簫簫 that the 簫簫 the fact that the plan fails to accomplish that, no one 簫簫 nobody can object?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Well, it is interesting that in this case neither the Virginia State House nor the Virginia State Senate, both of whom are controlled by the same party as the members of Congress affected, neither body nor the legislature as a whole has chosen to intervene in this case, which is quite different than, for example, in the Arizona case that this Court handled 簫簫 resolved where it found 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Under your view, could the legislature as a whole intervene?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: I think that the legislature could have intervened and would 簫簫 and would have a better claim to standing again under the 簫簫 the Arizona redistricting case that this Court decided.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So a legislature who passes the law has greater standing than the individual who is affected by the law?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Well, we are all affected by the law. I say "we" because I live in the Commonwealth. All Virginia voters are affected by the law, and members of Congress have to 簫簫 the 簫簫 the Appellants in this case have to do better than that. They can't just say they are affected by the law. They have to show why they have a legally protected interest as Justice Kagan 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: It sounds to me like it's incumbency, which is the very thing you say that this one individual cannot protect. Why can a legislature 簫簫 a whole legislature say it, but not one legislature. I don't understand it.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: So, Your Honor, one thing I want to clarify just from the record, it is not the case that we have conceded nor is it the case that the Court found, nor are the underlying facts of the case that the Virginia legislature endeavored to protect incumbents. What Mr. Janis said was quite specific. He did not want to pair incumbents. He did not want to draw them 簫簫 their houses out of their districts. That is quite different than saying that the Virginia legislature had a policy of protecting incumbents. In fact, the only way to protect incumbents would have been to use partisan data or race as a proxy for partisan data. The second of which would be unconstitutional 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Race 簫簫 you say race is a proxy. If 簫簫 and this is why, easily, I think it's so important. The way you check is to come up with a district that would achieve the same partisan objectives without respect to race, and you weren't put to that test in this case.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I 簫簫 I think that that is true in the circumstances in a case such as 簫簫 as Cromartie where the evidence, the direct evidence that race predominated was quite weak. In fact, it 簫簫 arguably the direct evidence went the other way, suggested that politics was what drove 簫簫 what drove the map. And the Court was 簫簫 was evaluating what do you do in a circumstance where there is no direct evidence that race predominated, but you have circumstantial evidence that maybe it did, maybe it didn't. And in that case, one way to tease out that 簫簫 whether it was partisanship or race 簫簫 is to say, okay, show me the map that teases that out. In this case, we have no need to tease it out. It 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No. I mean, it makes it 簫簫 I guess you have a greater degree of confidence if you have an alternative that said, look, if they wanted partisanship, which is usually a pretty high priority for politicians, if they wanted partisanship, they would have done this, but instead they did this. But if you're not 簫簫 if you're not forced to show that, then you have reliance on, you know, however many quotes you can find, and 簫簫 and I get back to the question I asked before. What do you 簫簫 how do you analyze it if it's 10 percent race, 10 percent partisanship and 80 percent who say nothing at all?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: So in this case, Your Honor, just as a factual matter, it was the person who sponsored the bill, the person who drew the map.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Is that Janis?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Janis.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: He wasn't in the legislature when this took 簫簫 was approved, was he?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: I believe he 簫簫 I believe he was at the 簫簫 at the time that the 簫簫 that the map was approved. He was the sponsor of the bill.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I thought the 簫簫 the person who drafted the plan 簫簫 maybe I've got that mixed up. It wasn't Janis 簫簫 was 簫簫 was not in the legislature.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Well, Janis says that he 簫簫 that he 簫簫 in fact, I believe said that he in fact drafted the 簫簫 we may be talking against each other about the house 簫簫 the State house plan versus the 簫簫 the congressional plan. But in any event, the 簫簫 the fact is that if you look at Exhibit 57, which Mr. Raphael pointed to, what you see is that Professor McDonald did a very important calculation. What he looked at is the 簫簫 the voters that were added and the voters that were 簫簫 that were taken out. And what he found is that the voters being added were much higher propensity 簫簫 had a higher percentage of black voters than they were Democratic. So in fact, he found 簫簫 to use a colloquialism, he found the blackest parts of the voters 簫簫 the voter pool, and added them, and skipped over white Democratic voters instead. The differential was about 2簫1. So in fact, there was an analysis that teased out based on statistics.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So you could have 簫簫 you think you could have drawn a map under Cromartie that would show if you wanted to protect incumbency or 簫簫 and Republican or Democratic advantage, you would have done this, and instead you did this?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: I don't think that that 簫簫 that the 簫簫 the question is whether we could have drawn a map. The question is whether or not Cromartie requires, in a case where 簫簫 where 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, why isn't it the question where you could have drawn the map another way? Are 簫簫 are you saying that you can draw a map only by using race, then you can't draw the map? Is that your position? I can see where you could take that position, but is that what you're saying?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Yeah. Well, my position is that as 簫簫 as Mr. Raphael suggested, the analysis that was done involved a handful of VTDs. So I 簫簫 which are essentially precincts. So we could have potentially drawn, after that analysis, a district that made very few changes. But I don't think that that's what Cromartie had in mind. I think Cromartie had in mind a circumstance where you are not using race as a proxy, and you are drawing a significantly different district that shows that race and 簫簫 that race predominated over partisanship. Whereas, in this case the use of these VTDs was to try to get at something different, which was the intent of what Mr. Janis and the legislature had in mind.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let's not forget that there was 簫簫 counsel, I'm over here 簫簫 you're skipping over it. And I'll bet the map might have been slightly different. It still was going to be different 簫簫

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Yes, it was 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 if you had not used race.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's the whole purpose of the exercise, correct?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If you're race neutral, you move people not on the basis of their skin color, but on some 簫簫 some neutral principle. And you have shown that in at least five precincts were moved where it wasn't on the base of partisanship, it was on the basis of race.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but if you moved those districts, then you'd have to move other districts to make up for 簫簫 for it. And again, I think that's what Cromartie does. It says we don't have to speculate about 10 percent, 10 percent, 80 percent. What you have to show is that partisanship could not have been a factor, because you couldn't have drawn it any differently without affecting partisanship.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: I think that our burden was to show that race predominated. And I don't think that Cromartie puts a straightjacket on Miller and Vera to say that the only way 簫簫 or Shaw 簫簫 that the only way you can do that is through the alternative map.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: If 簫簫 if race and partisanship are co簫extensive, which one predominates?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Well, in a case where the legislature tells you?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, no. If race and partisanship are co簫extensive, then which one 簫簫 it's 簫簫 you may say it's an abstract question that isn't that the same but doesn't fit these facts 簫簫 but if that's the case, which one predominates?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: If 簫簫 if you had a circumstance where there was no other evidence other than these two factors, race and 簫簫 and partisanship, then essentially it's a tie, then neither predominates over the other.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And who loses if it's a tie?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: We would lose 簫簫 we would lose if it's a tie, but in this case there is no tie. There's nothing even approaching a tie. The legislature set a 55 percent threshold.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Suppose the legislature had set the same number as what was referred to as the Benchmark Plan. Suppose, instead of making it 56.3 they kept it at 53.1, kept it exactly the same as in the 簫簫 the prior plan.

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, if it was done as a mechanical threshold, then it would be subject to heightened scrutiny. The State would have to show that 簫簫 that it had met that burden. Any time the State sorts people based on race in a 簫簫 using mechanical targets or 簫簫 or thresholds in a redistricting context, then it has to show that there was a 簫簫 a very good reason for doing so 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is that what you're making it right on? Do you agree with the Solicitor General that a simple statement from the line drawers that they were trying the best they can to comply with the Voting Rights Act, that that is not sufficient to 簫簫 to have strict scrutiny apply?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: So I think that this Court in Alabama made clear that the fact that the State of Virginia may have been under the mistaken belief 簫簫 a good faith mistaken belief 簫簫 that it had to 簫簫 that it had to go to 55 percent 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes. No. I got that. I'm saying assume a different 簫簫 a different set of facts where they weren't just saying we have to stick at 53.1, or we have to go at 55.55 percent. Assume a different state of facts where line drawers simply say of course our first priority is to comply with the law. Do you think that that itself triggers strict scrutiny?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: No, of course not. That's the Supremacy Clause. So the fact that the 簫簫 the fact that the State of Virginia understood it needed to comply with the Voting Rights Act does not, in and of itself, trigger 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So what you think triggers strict scrutiny is essentially the use of a mechanical target here?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Correct. Correct.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: It's not related to the ability to elect?

turns.name: MARC E. ELIAS
turns.text: Correct. And in fact, if you look at what Professor Grofman did as the Court 簫簫 as the Court's Special Master, you can see he did a very thoughtful analysis that weighed all of the traditional redistricting criteria, and then looked at the impact that it would have on the ability of African簫Americans to elect a candidate of choice. And that is a model of the kind of analysis that 簫簫 that the State of Virginia should have engaged in but didn't. Instead, it started with a 50. It said the district was 53.1. If we go under 53.1, we are breaking the law. Let's go to 55, because that gives us certainty rather than a lack of certainty. So 55 percent it is.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Gershengorn. FOR UNITED STATES, AS AMICUS CURIAE, SUPPORTING APPELLEES

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to make two points on the merits, but I'd like to start where this Court is going to start, with standing. We believe that Appellants lack standing to appeal. Appellants allege that the district court's judgment may cause them harm by adding voters to the district of a different political party who may vote against them. But candidates have no legally簫cognizable interest in the particular composition of the voters in their district 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is that 簫簫 is that true when the legislature specifically has adopted incumbency protection as a matter of State law or a matter of State policy?

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: So Your Honor, I think if they had adopted, as a matter of State law, incumbency protection, that might be different, because then the legislature would have established that, actually, as a legally簫cognizable right. But that's not what's at interest here. And if I could say, Your Honor, what's at 簫簫 what's at issue here is whether a candidate has an interest in a particular composition of his voters. And we think actually Your Honor's own opinion in Lulac is quite instructive on this. What Your Honor said in Lulac, if you'll recall, is Congressman Bonilla had alleged that the allegation was that the Latino voters there were no longer voting for Congressman Bonilla, and that was why they redistricted. And what you 簫簫 what your opinion for the Court said there was that that kind of voter protection, which is for the candidate and not for 簫簫 not for the individual voter, is fine for the realm of politics, but it did not justify the 簫簫 could not justify the action there to save it for Section 2. That's the same point.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's the area District 5, District 5 in the State which is heavily African簫American and so imagined racist legislatures changed the whole district so they couldn't possibly elect an African簫American. Does the African簫American member of Congress have standing to contest that?

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: So a voter would have standing to contest it. A candidate would have standing to contest it if he or she were a voter in the district. I think under Hays, that 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And as a congressman, not.

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: As a congressman, I think not, at least this Court has not said. But remember, what we have here is a quite different situation where you have a candidate 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, I know it's different, but what I'm looking for throughout is the 簫簫 I understand that there has been no case which discusses this 簫簫 that I've been able to find. They have the Meese case, which is in a different context, but why is that different? And what's bothering me about it, which 簫簫 I don't want you to just say I'm right; I want you to explain why, if I a.m., or why I'm wrong 簫簫 is that there are potentially dozens of remedial plans and there are hundreds of possible plans for a State, and every plan will hurt someone. And if one district in a State is changed, suddenly you open the door to every legislature and every Congressman from every other district challenging the plan. That strikes me as a big shift in the direction of taking power from the legislature and turning it over to the judges as to what kind of districting plan you're going to have, and a mess to boot.

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's what's worrying me.

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: And I think you're right to be worried about it, and I 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I knew you thought I would be, given your comments.

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: Now I'm going to tell you why. See 簫簫 but I 簫簫 and I do think there are a couple of responses to that. I mean, we do normally rely, in that instance, on the State to be the principal defender. But when the State is not there, what this Court recognized in Hollingsworth is often that means that the 簫簫 the bill goes undefended, and that's not something that concerns the Court. Now, the reason why we have to be very careful about legislators and why choosing their own 簫簫 I think, as plaintiffs' counsel said, we don't usually let legislators choose their own 簫簫 choose their own voters, and there is good reason for that. That's not the way the system is supposed to work. And I do think it would have quite expansive effects. It's not clear to us there is a huge difference between this kind of line簫drawing and a challenge; for example, that a legislator might seek to appeal the relocation of a base or a university in his or her district on the grounds that that would radically change the number of Republican or Democratic voters in the district. We do think Meese is 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But if you 簫簫 yeah, I 簫簫 I'm sorry. I 簫簫

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: I was just going to say that we think Meese is very different, because Meese is not about choosing the voters in the district. It's about 簫簫 we're not saying that you don't have an injury 簫簫 Article III harm from a harm to reelection. We are saying that you don't have an interest in vindicating that right through a 簫簫 through choosing the voters in your district. I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess I find this a little bit harder than you just suggested, because this is not Representative Forbes saying, I want to choose exactly the set of voters that's going to increase my own electoral chances. This is Representative Forbes saying, look, there has been an act of the legislature, and the 簫簫 the act of the legislature has given me a certain set of voters, and why don't I have a legally cognizable interest in relying on that legislative judgment when some court has taken it away?

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: Your Honor, I think it's for the same reason that this Court rejected that idea in the 簫簫 in Hays itself. There isn't a 簫簫 a 簫簫 a cognizable interest among the voters or among the candidates in just seeing that a 簫簫 a lawfully legislated districting plan is enacted. Otherwise, I think every voter in the State would have standing, because the legislature said, you should be in this district, you should have a fair opportunity to vote. But that's not the direction the Court has gone. And I do think that the combination of 簫簫 viewing the office as, one, that the officeholder gets to choose the constituents, and the potentially broad impact of that is one that should give this Court some pause. And particularly 簫簫 again, just to pick up on what plaintiffs' 簫簫 plaintiffs' counsel, Mr. Elias, was saying 簫簫 I think, particularly in a situation where you have a State statute, in this Court's observation in Hollingsworth, it would be quite unusual, I think, to find standing here. If I could switch over to the merits very quickly, a couple of 簫簫 a couple of points. I wanted to start with the observations of Justice Kennedy and Justice Kagan on the could簫have, would簫have standard that Mr. Carvin has 簫簫 has put forth, which, as I understand it, is basically, the district is okay even if based on race as long as it could have been drawn on the basis of politics or would have been. We think that really flies in the face of the Shaw and Miller line of cases, that what those cases are about at core are two principal things: That you can't use race as a proxy, and you can't sort voters on the basis of race. And when you do that, it is not a defense to say, well, I could have done the same thing on the basis of politics. You can send it back. If the legislature, in fact, does the same thing, taking race out of the equation, then fine. The injury that the Shaw line of cases was designed to get at is eliminated. It is precisely the sorting. And so I think the would簫have or could簫have test that Mr. Carvin has put forward is really quite at odds with 簫簫 with the 簫簫 with this Court's jurisprudence.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But, I mean, people have objected to some extent that Cromartie cut back on Shaw and Miller as well, and again, I just 簫簫 I, at least, would feel on much more solid ground if the plaintiffs had been put to the test of saying, show us. They say this isn't about partisanship; this is about race. Okay. Show us. You draw the district that would protect the partisanship interest that's going to be different. And yet the 簫簫 the lower court did not subject them to that inquiry.

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: So, Your Honor, we think Cromartie is a very important case, but it actually is quite the 簫簫 the exact opposite of the situation here. We think Cromartie is the situation in which the legislator 簫簫 there was direct and substantial evidence that the legislature acted on the basis of politics, and statistics were put forward that said it's equally explained by race. And what this Court said, and we think it was sensible, is there's basically a thumb on the scale for politics at that point, to give the State legislatures their room. But in a situation like this, where there is direct and substantial evidence that race was at issue, that same evidence that it's equally consistent with politics just doesn't cut it. That 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, okay, if there is evidence that race is at issue. I will give you a chance to answer the question I asked each of the others. I wouldn't want to deprive you of that opportunity.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But you're looking to see whether race was the motive. What do you do if, as I said, you know, 10 say yes, 10 say something else, and 80 don't say anything? How can you say that the motive of the legislature was 簫簫 was this or that?

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: So, Your Honor, I think 簫簫 it's obviously a difficult question, but I would say two things on that. First is, this Court's cases have been fairly unanimous in looking to the 簫簫 to the intent of the drafter. That's what they look to, for example, in Bush v. Vera. And in Alabama, this Court had a policy, and it didn't look to see whether each of the legislators individually had embraced that policy. That was something that the Court accepted. So I think there is a long line of case law going that way. And second, of course, it's not solely the intent of the drafter here. There are objective indicators, which this Court has indicated in both Shaw and Miller are extremely important, things the Court looks to 簫簫 the traditional redistricting factors, such as contiguity, compactness, are counties being split 簫簫 that reinforce that kind of intent. And those are things that are open, that 簫簫 that are 簫簫 are part of the plan that was enacted by all of the legislature. And here 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: What if the drafter or other members of the legislature say, race was our first consideration, and by that, what we mean is that we have to take race into account under the Voting Rights Act, and that's what we've done? Would that 簫簫 what would be the 簫簫 what would be the result there?

turns.name: IAN H. GERSHENGORN
turns.text: So, Your Honor, I don't think that that necessarily results in strict scrutiny. What this Court has said over and over is that the 簫簫 that race must predominate. The mere intentionally 簫簫 or consciousness of race, or even the intentional use of race, is not sufficient. And we think that makes sense, because as the Court has said, the redistricters are always conscious of race and always aware of race, and that the State legislatures need room and 簫簫 need room to maneuver. And so the mere 簫簫 the mere fact that you're conscious of race or even that race 簫簫 you intentionally use race is not sufficient by itself to have strict scrutiny.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Carvin, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: I'd like to begin with Justice Kagan's questions. We've heard a constant theme that if it was done because of race, a post hoc political explanation doesn't justify it 簫簫 I fully agree with that 簫簫 with a mixed motive case here. That's what the district court said: Race, politics, and incumbency protection. And the only reason that race was, quote, "ranked higher" was because he said that it was a Federal mandate under the Supremacy Clause. So if that's not a justification, they committed legal error. If you turn to 33a, why was politics and incumbency subordinate? They told you: Because that, quote, "goal was permissive and subordinate to the mandatory criteria of compliance with the VRA." Now, they said that they implemented it by not reducing the benchmark BVAP, but that had nothing to do with the rank ordering of VRA over these other things. To get to your question: If two 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I take the point, Mr. Carvin, but isn't that really exactly what we confronted in Alabama, which is 簫簫 you know, the number one priority was the VRA, but then it turned out that they had misunderstood the VIA, so it turned out that the number one priority was a racial quota, which had nothing to do with the way the VRA is really supposed to operate?

turns.name: MICHAEL A. CARVIN
turns.text: That's fine, and that goes to narrow tailoring, but what we are trying to figure out here is whether or not there is a prima facie case. Now, assume with me that in 30 of the districts in Alabama, race was completely coextensive. They didn't assert politics, but with county lines. Would you ever say that race predominated over something or subordinated something when they are entirely coextensive? Let's take it out of the racial context. Compactness is number two; county lines are number three. You draw a nice, compact district that complies with county lines. No one in their right mind would say "compactness predominated over county lines" because the same result was ordained by these two motives. And you can search this opinion for any finding that race was inconsistent with or subordinated incumbency protection or politics, and you won't find it. Therefore, they haven't made their basic burden of showing that traditional districting principles were subordinated nor their specific Cromartie II burden of showing it was subordinated to race rather than politics. The only evidence that they have even tried to come up with at the last minute is Joint Appendix 439. This is the VTDL analysis done by McDonald. It is undisputed that it has exactly the same flaws that this Court rejected as a matter of law in Cromartie II. Why? Because the racial effect is identical to the political effect. He made a big deal about the fact that there was a 16.5 percent gap between the VTDs in District 3 and those outside of District 3 in terms of race. But what his own index shows on JA 439 is there was also a 16 percent gap in Democratic percentages. So it's exactly the same flaw that was at issue in Cromartie II is here. So unless this Court is prepared to allow district courts to engage in naked defiance of the plain language and holding of Cromartie II, this case needs to be reversed. As to your direct evidence point, Justice Kagan, what was the direct evidence in Cromartie II? Partisan and racial balance. The Court said as clear as possible since he said partisanship and race, it says little or nothing about the relative predominance. What do we have here? Incumbency protection, politics and race. Therefore the direct evidence says little or nothing about the relative predominance. What you need to do is to show that they could have accomplished their legitimate political objectives in some other way. As to standing, I've heard the slogan repeated by all of my opponents, "voters choose representatives not vice versa." That's a lovely slogan. But the relevant point here is that State legislatures choose which districts those voters go into, not the Federal judiciary. If, as you must assume, the Federal judiciary has exceeded its proper role and created a system which dramatically hurts the incumbents who were designed to be protected by this law, how can they not have a legally cognizable injury簫in簫fact?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
